Maladaptive Strategies.value: Seeing other people that were involved in the event or that look similar
I try to erase the memory of the event
I think about how life would have been different if the event had not occurred

Threat.value: Irritable behavior, angry outbursts, or acting aggressively
Being superalert or watchful or on guard

Memory.value: My memories of the frightening event are like a film that plays over and over.
When memories come to mind of what happened, I feel my body is in the same position as when the frightening event occurred.
When I have memories of what happened I sometimes hear things in my head that I heard during the frightening event.

Negative Appraisals.value: I can't trust that I will do the right thing
I used to be a happy person but now I am always miserable.
I have permanently changed for the worse

issues.item: Crash Imagery: Cars hit by accident
issues.explanation: Police trauma type doesn't naturally connect to car crash imagery. While police encounters could involve vehicles, 'cars hit by accident' suggests traffic collisions rather than police-related incidents, creating a thematic mismatch with the trauma type.

Police trauma doesn't inherently involve military themes. While some police operations might resemble military tactics, this trigger seems more aligned with combat trauma than police encounters, creating inconsistency with the trauma type.

issues.item: Photographic Recall: Remembering what happened during the frightening event is just like looking at photographs of it in my mind.
issues.explanation: Direct contradiction with 'Auditory (Language Confusion): Uncomprehinsible chatter' trigger. Photographic recall implies clear, detailed visual memory, while language confusion suggests auditory processing difficulties during the event. These cannot plausibly coexist in the same trauma memory.

vignette:
Rohan, a 74-year-old non-binary South Asian person, presents with significant distress following a traumatic encounter with police. Their life is now punctuated by moments of intense fear and re-experiencing. The sight of a uniformed officer or the sharp smell of alcohol can instantly transport them back to the event, their body tensing into the same defensive posture it held that night. These triggers also bring on a flood of sensory memory: the chaotic, incomprehensible chatter of the officers plays on a loop in their mind, a relentless auditory recall that feels as real as the original moment.

In response to these overwhelming intrusions, Rohan has structured their life around avoidance. They actively steer clear of anyone who reminds them of the people involved, altering routes and social plans. When the memories surge, they engage in a futile internal battle, trying desperately to erase the mental film that replays without warning. This effort is often accompanied by counterfactual thinking, as they obsess over how different—and happier—their life might have been.

The constant state of alarm is exhausting. Rohan struggles with sleep, lying awake hypervigilant to every sound, and their patience is worn thin, leading to irritable outbursts that strain their relationship. This pervasive sense of threat, fueled by the recurring memories and triggered by environmental cues, further compels their avoidant strategies, creating a cycle of withdrawal.

Beneath this cycle lies a profound shift in their self-view. Rohan feels they are no longer the happy person they once were, convinced this change is permanent and for the worse. A deep-seated self-doubt has taken root, making them question their every decision. Their world has narrowed, defined by the memories they cannot escape and the triggers they cannot always avoid.
